walking.label: Walk Ease
walking.subtitle: Pre-Walk, Post-Walk, Reset
walking.purpose: Wake the gait system before the miles, then decompress calves, quads, and arches after.

sections.label: Pre-Walk: The Gait Prep
sections.purpose: Wake ankles, hips, and arms before a long walk.

exercises.name: Ankle Circles
exercises.start: Standing tall, using a wall for balance if needed.
exercises.sub: Mobilize the ankles before the miles.

steps.cue: Lift one foot off the ground while lightly supporting yourself on the wall if needed.
Rotate the ankle in slow, controlled clockwise circles.
Switch to counter-clockwise circles, then repeat with the other foot.

exercises.name: Glute Activation Drive
exercises.start: Standing with feet hip-width apart.
exercises.sub: Fire the glutes to support the pelvis with each step.

steps.cue: Shift weight to your left leg and engage your core.
Lift the right knee toward your chest while squeezing the left glute.
Lower the foot with control and repeat on the other side, staying tall.

exercises.name: Controlled Arm Swings
exercises.start: Standing tall with shoulders relaxed.
exercises.sub: Rehearse efficient arm swing for walking.

steps.cue: Bend your elbows slightly and let the arms hang by your sides.
Swing your arms forward and backward from the shoulder joint in a natural rhythm.
Keep your neck long and avoid shrugging the shoulders as you move.

sections.label: Post-Walk: The Decompression
sections.purpose: Release calves, quads, and foot arches after the miles.

exercises.name: Calf Release
exercises.start: Standing facing a wall.
exercises.sub: Lengthen the calf and Achilles after long walks.

steps.cue: Place both hands on the wall and step one foot back.
Keep the back heel on the floor and lean into the wall until you feel a stretch in the calf.
Slightly bend the back knee to target the lower calf and Achilles, then switch sides.

exercises.name: Standing Quad Stretch
exercises.start: Standing tall, holding a chair for balance.
exercises.sub: Open the front of the thigh after steady forward motion.

steps.cue: Reach back and grab your right ankle or foot.
Pull the heel toward your glute while keeping your knees together and torso upright.
Hold the stretch, then repeat on the left side.

exercises.name: Foot Arch Massage
exercises.start: Standing or seated.
exercises.sub: Reset the arches after time on your feet.

steps.cue: Place a small ball or firm object under the arch of your foot.
Apply gentle pressure and roll the foot forward and backward over the object.
Spend extra time on any tight or tender spots before switching feet.

running.label: Run Reboot
running.subtitle: Impact, Hips, Recovery
running.purpose: Prime tendons and hips before impact, then restore structure and circulation post-run.

sections.label: Pre-Run: The Impact Ready
sections.purpose: Prime tendons, hips, and hamstrings before impact.

exercises.name: Pogo Hops
exercises.start: Standing with feet hip-width apart.
exercises.sub: Wake up calf and Achilles for impact.

steps.cue: Keep your knees almost straight and stand tall.
Jump using only your ankles and calves, staying on the balls of your feet.
Maintain a quick, rhythmic pace to prime the tendons.

exercises.name: Runner's Lunge with Reach
exercises.start: Standing tall.
exercises.sub: Open hip flexors and side body before the run.

steps.cue: Step back into a lunge, keeping the back leg straight and front knee stacked over the ankle.
Reach both arms toward the ceiling and tilt your torso slightly away from the back leg.
Return to standing and switch sides with control.

exercises.name: Hamstring Scoops
exercises.start: Standing with one heel forward and toes pointed up.
exercises.sub: Lengthen the hamstrings and groove front-leg loading.

steps.cue: Hinge at the hips while keeping the front leg straight and back knee soft.
Reach down and scoop your hands toward the floor in front of the foot, then up overhead.
Take a small step forward and repeat on the other leg.

sections.label: Post-Run: The Structural Reset
sections.purpose: Open hips, front body, and assist venous return after the run.

exercises.name: Seated Figure-4
exercises.start: Seated on the floor with legs extended.
exercises.sub: Decompress the hips after repetitive strides.

steps.cue: Bend your left knee and cross your right ankle over the left thigh.
Lean forward slightly or pull the left thigh toward you to feel the stretch in the right hip.
Hold, then repeat on the opposite side.

exercises.name: Cobra Pose
exercises.start: Lying face down on a mat.
exercises.sub: Restore extension through the front of the body.

steps.cue: Place your hands on the floor next to your shoulders with elbows close to your sides.
Gently press through your hands to lift your chest off the floor, keeping your hips grounded.
Draw shoulders away from your ears and breathe into the front of the body.

exercises.name: Legs Up the Wall
exercises.start: Lying on your back with your glutes as close to a wall as possible.
exercises.sub: Assist circulation and recovery after impact.

steps.cue: Extend your legs straight up against the wall with heels resting lightly.
Let your arms rest by your sides with palms up and jaw unclenched.
Stay here for about two minutes, breathing slowly to calm the system.

golf.label: Swing Sync
golf.subtitle: Golf, Rotation, Ready
golf.purpose: Prepare rotation, wrists, and hips before the first tee, then decompress after the final putt.

sections.label: Pre-Game: The Dynamic Drive
sections.purpose: Prime rotation, wrists, and hips before stepping onto the first tee.

exercises.name: Standing Club Rotations
exercises.start: Standing tall with feet shoulder-width apart.
exercises.sub: Separate upper body rotation from stable hips.

steps.cue: Hold a golf club across your upper back and shoulders, gripping each end. Keep your spine straight and knees slightly bent.
Rotate your torso to the right as far as comfortable while keeping your hips and feet facing forward.
Slowly rotate to the left, feeling the separation between your upper and lower body.

exercises.name: Leg Swings
exercises.start: Standing near a wall or golf cart for balance.
exercises.sub: Lubricate the hip joint in front, back, and lateral planes.

steps.cue: Stand on one leg, engage your core, and rest a hand lightly on the wall or cart for balance.
Swing the opposite leg forward and backward in a controlled motion, gradually increasing the range as the hip warms up.
Switch to side-to-side swings in front of your body to open the hip socket in the lateral plane.

exercises.name: Active Wrist Circles
exercises.start: Arms extended in front of you at chest height.
exercises.sub: Warm wrists and forearms for gripping the club.

steps.cue: Make light fists with both hands with arms extended at shoulder height.
Rotate your wrists in slow, controlled clockwise circles, using the full available range.
Change direction to counter-clockwise, releasing tension through the forearms.

sections.label: Post-Game: The Fairway Recovery
sections.purpose: Decompress the spine, hips, and shoulders after a full round.

exercises.name: Open Books
exercises.start: Lying on your side, knees stacked and bent at 90 degrees.
exercises.sub: Restore thoracic rotation after repetitive swings.

steps.cue: Extend both arms straight out in front of you with palms touching and knees stacked.
Lift the top arm and rotate it slowly across your body toward the floor behind you while keeping your knees pinned together.
Breathe deeply into the stretch, then slowly return to the starting position and repeat.

exercises.name: Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch
exercises.start: In a half-kneeling position with one knee on the floor.
exercises.sub: Lengthen the front of the hip after walking and swinging.

steps.cue: Keep your front foot flat, torso upright, and ribs stacked over the pelvis.
Gently tuck your pelvis under and lean forward slightly until you feel a stretch in the front of your back hip.
Reach the arm on the same side as your back knee toward the ceiling to deepen the release.

exercises.name: Thread the Needle
exercises.start: On all fours with hands under shoulders and knees under hips.
exercises.sub: Release tension between the shoulder blades.

steps.cue: Slide one arm underneath the other, palm up, reaching as far as comfortable.
Lower your shoulder and ear toward the floor, letting the upper back rotate.
Hold and breathe into the space between your shoulder blades before switching sides.

tennis.label: Court Prep
tennis.subtitle: Racket, Shoulders, Hips
tennis.purpose: Ready the lateral base and shoulders for court work, then release forearms, hips, and side body after play.

sections.label: Pre-Game: The Court Readiness
sections.purpose: Prepare hips, shoulders, and ankles for multi-directional court work.

exercises.name: Lateral Lunges with Reach
exercises.start: Standing with feet wide apart.
exercises.sub: Groove lateral load and balance for wide court coverage.

steps.cue: Shift your weight to the right, bending the right knee while keeping the left leg straight.
Reach your arms forward to balance your weight and keep the chest lifted.
Push off the right foot to return to center, then repeat on the left side.

exercises.name: Shoulder Y & T Raises
exercises.start: Standing with a slight hinge at the hips.
exercises.sub: Activate lower traps and posterior shoulder for overhead work.

steps.cue: Let your arms hang down with thumbs pointing out and spine long.
Raise your arms up to form a Y shape, squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Lower your arms, then raise them straight out to the sides to form a T, maintaining control.

exercises.name: Ankle Hops
exercises.start: Standing tall with feet close together.
exercises.sub: Prime calf and Achilles for quick first steps.

steps.cue: Stay light on the balls of your feet with knees slightly soft.
Perform small, quick vertical hops, focusing on a fast bounce off the ground.
Keep your core tight and land softly each time without letting the heels slam down.

sections.label: Post-Game: The Decompression
sections.purpose: Release forearms, hips, and side body after play.

exercises.name: Forearm Extensor Release
exercises.start: Arm extended straight in front of you.
exercises.sub: Unload the outer elbow and backhand tissues.

steps.cue: Point your fingers toward the floor with the palm facing you.
Use the opposite hand to gently pull the back of your hand toward your body until you feel a stretch.
Hold for several breaths, then ease off and switch sides.

exercises.name: Figure-4 Glute Stretch
exercises.start: Lying on your back with knees bent.
exercises.sub: Open the hips after repeated lunges and changes of direction.

steps.cue: Cross your right ankle over your left knee to create a figure-4 shape.
Reach through and grab your left thigh, gently pulling it toward your chest.
Hold for several breaths, then repeat on the opposite side.

exercises.name: Child's Pose with Side Reach
exercises.start: Kneeling on the floor, sitting back on your heels.
exercises.sub: Lengthen lats and side body after serving and overheads.

steps.cue: Fold forward and rest your forehead on the floor with arms extended in front of you.
Walk both hands to the right until you feel a stretch along your left side and lat.
Walk hands back to center, then over to the left side and repeat.

thoracic.label: Thoracic Chest Reset
thoracic.subtitle: Mobility, Upper Back, Chest
thoracic.purpose: A guided 7–10 minute flow to restore thoracic mobility and reduce upper back tension.

sections.label: Thoracic Flow
sections.purpose: A short guided sequence to restore thoracic mobility, reduce upper back tension, and relieve chest/arm referral. Works well before/after sitting or anxious states.

exercises.name: Thoracic Extension (foam roller)
exercises.start: Seated or supine with foam roller/rolled towel under the upper back (shoulder blade level).

steps.label: Setup
steps.cue: Place foam roller or rolled towel under upper back. Keep knees bent and feet on the floor if lying.

steps.label: Extend
steps.cue: Support your head with hands and gently arch back over the roller. Pause and breathe for 3-5 breaths.

steps.label: Alternative
steps.cue: If no roller, use a chair back, wall, or puppy-pose quadruped variation to find comfortable extension.

exercises.name: Cat–Cow
exercises.start: On all fours, neutral spine.

steps.label: Neutral Tabletop
steps.cue: Hands under shoulders, knees under hips. Neutral spine, neck long.

steps.label: Cow — Inhale
steps.cue: Inhale, drop the belly, lift the chest and tailbone.

steps.label: Cat — Exhale
steps.cue: Exhale, round through the spine segment by segment. Move slowly for 10 cycles.

exercises.name: Thoracic Rotation
exercises.start: Seated or kneeling with arms crossed.

steps.label: Seated Setup
steps.cue: Sit tall with hands across chest or arms hugging shoulders.

steps.label: Rotate
steps.cue: Rotate upper body gently to one side, keeping hips facing forward. Return and repeat other side.
Keep the movement in your upper back only. Hips stay forward-facing throughout.

exercises.name: Wall Angels
exercises.start: Standing with back against a wall.

steps.label: Wall Contact
steps.cue: Head, upper back and glutes touch the wall. Arms in a W at shoulder height.

steps.label: Slide to Y
steps.cue: Slide arms up overhead into a Y keeping contact with the wall. Return slowly.
Quality over range. If contact with the wall is lost, reduce your range of motion.

exercises.name: Chin Tucks
exercises.start: Seated or standing tall.

steps.label: Setup
steps.cue: Sit or stand tall, eyes forward.

steps.label: Tuck
steps.cue: Draw chin straight back (not down), hold gently for 3–5 seconds then release.
You should feel a gentle stretch at the base of the skull. No tilting up or down.

exercises.name: Thread the Needle
exercises.start: All fours — hands under shoulders, knees under hips.

steps.label: Setup
steps.cue: From tabletop, reach one arm under the body and rest shoulder on the floor.

steps.label: Hold
steps.cue: Breathe here 20–30 seconds per side and feel rotation through the upper back.
Let gravity do the work. Do not force the shoulder toward the floor.

exercises.name: Child's Pose
exercises.start: Kneel and reach arms forward.

steps.label: Kneel & Fold
steps.cue: Sit back toward heels and fold forward with arms extended. Relax the chest toward the floor.
Breathe deeply into the lower back. Let your chest and shoulders soften toward the floor. Hold for the full duration.
Widen your knees if you need more room for your torso.

wrist.label: Carpal Tunnel Release
wrist.subtitle: Glides, Stretches, Nerves
wrist.purpose: Short PT-style routine for wrist and hand mobility — perfect for keyboard users.

sections.label: Wrist & Hand Reset
sections.purpose: Short PT-style routine: glides, stretches and nerve glides for wrist and hand health — great for keyboard users.

exercises.name: Wrist Flexor Stretch
exercises.start: Seated or standing, arm extended.

steps.label: Setup
steps.cue: Extend the arm with palm facing up.

steps.label: Stretch
steps.cue: Use the other hand to gently pull fingers back toward you. Hold 30s per side.
Never force the stretch. A gentle pull is correct — sharp pain or tingling means reduce the range.

exercises.name: Wrist Extensor Stretch
exercises.start: Arm extended palm down.

steps.label: Setup
steps.cue: Extend the arm with palm facing down.

steps.label: Stretch
steps.cue: Use the other hand to press the fingers toward the forearm. Hold 30s per side.
Keep the elbow straight for maximum stretch. Reduce range if you feel tingling in the fingers.

exercises.name: Median Nerve Glide
exercises.start: Seated, arm supported.

steps.label: Setup
steps.cue: Start with a neutral wrist and fingers.

steps.label: Glide
steps.cue: Make a fist, then straighten fingers and thumb across the palm. Repeat smoothly for 10 reps.
Move slowly and smoothly. These are glides, not stretches — do not force the range.

exercises.name: Prayer Stretch
exercises.start: Standing or seated at chest height.

steps.label: Prayer Setup
steps.cue: Bring palms together at chest, fingers pointing up.

steps.label: Lower
steps.cue: Slowly lower hands while keeping palms together to stretch the wrists. Hold 20s.
Go only as far as is comfortable. Stop if you feel tingling or numbness.

exercises.name: Shakeout & Fist Pumps
exercises.start: Standing or seated.

steps.label: Shake
steps.cue: Shake both hands loose for 15 seconds.

steps.label: Fist Pumps
steps.cue: Alternate making a fist and opening the hand with punch-like pumps for 15 seconds.
End every session with this. It flushes tension from the hands and signals the nervous system to reset.

anxiety.label: Calm Circuit
anxiety.subtitle: Breathe, Ground, Focus
anxiety.purpose: Evidence-based techniques that directly shift the nervous system from fight-or-flight to rest and digest.

sections.label: Immediate Nervous System Reset
sections.purpose: Anxiety floods the system with adrenaline and cortisol, shifting the nervous system into sympathetic overdrive. These first four techniques work fast — each one has a direct, measurable effect on heart rate, vagal tone, or the threat-detection centers of the brain. Use them as soon as you feel the activation rising.

exercises.name: Cold Shock (Dive Reflex)
exercises.start: Standing or seated near a sink or bowl of cold water.
exercises.sub: Rapid parasympathetic activation via the mammalian dive reflex

steps.label: Prepare Cold Water
steps.cue: Fill a sink or bowl with cold water — as cold as possible, ideally with ice. The mammalian dive reflex is one of the fastest known parasympathetic pathways: cold water on the face instantly slows the heart via the vagus nerve. Athletes and therapists use this for acute anxiety.

steps.label: Submerge Face or Wrists
steps.cue: Submerge your face for 15–30 seconds (hold breath), or hold both wrists under cold running water. If face submersion isn't possible, press a cold wet cloth firmly to the forehead and cheeks. The trigeminal nerve responds immediately.

steps.label: Surface and Breathe
steps.cue: Come up slowly and take a long, slow exhale. You will notice a distinct drop in heart rate and a shift in mental clarity — the hallmark of the dive reflex activating. Repeat 1–2 times if needed. This technique is a first-line DBT skill for acute emotional dysregulation.

exercises.name: Legs Up the Wall
exercises.start: Seated on the floor close to a wall, about to lie back.
exercises.sub: Passive inversion that shifts blood pooling and calms the heart

steps.label: Scoot to the Wall
steps.cue: Sit sideways next to a wall, then swing the legs up as you lie back. Legs rest against the wall, hips as close to the baseboard as comfortable. A folded blanket under the hips creates more traction if you have tight hamstrings. The key is legs above heart level.

steps.label: Settle and Release
steps.cue: Let the legs go heavy against the wall. Arms rest wide at 30°, palms up. Close the eyes. Passive inversion shifts venous return and activates baroreceptors in the neck and chest — the brain interprets this as cardiovascular calm. Heart rate measurably decreases within 2 minutes.

steps.label: Breathe Into the Belly
steps.cue: With gravity assisting, the diaphragm moves more freely here. Each breath goes deeper without effort. Stay for the full 2 minutes — anxiety often lifts noticeably within that window. When you come down, roll to one side and pause for 30 seconds before sitting up.

exercises.name: Voo Sounding
exercises.start: Seated or lying down, eyes closed, spine supported.
exercises.sub: Sub-diaphragmatic resonance for vagal nerve toning

steps.label: Settle and Find the Tone
steps.cue: Sit or lie comfortably. Take a full belly breath in. As you exhale, produce a slow, deep 'VooooOOOOO' sound — like a foghorn or a ship's horn. The key is that it resonates in the chest and belly, not just the throat. You should feel the vibration in the sternum and upper abdomen.

steps.label: Sustain for Full Exhale
steps.cue: Make the Voo last the entire exhale — 6–8 seconds minimum. The low-frequency vibration directly stimulates the vagus nerve through thoracic resonance. Dr. Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, uses this as a primary nervous system regulation tool.

steps.label: Pause and Inhale
steps.cue: After each Voo, rest for 2 breaths before the next one. Notice the quality of stillness between sounds — this is the parasympathetic response deepening. 6–8 rounds is a complete cycle. The effect is cumulative: each round deepens the shift.

exercises.name: 5-4-3-2-1 Sensory Scan
exercises.start: Seated upright, both feet flat on the floor.
exercises.sub: Sensory grounding that interrupts anxious cognitive loops

steps.label: Name 5 Things You See
steps.cue: Look around the room slowly. Name 5 distinct things you can see — out loud or in your mind. Describe them specifically: not just 'a chair' but 'a dark wooden chair with a cracked armrest.' Specificity matters. This engages the prefrontal cortex and begins to quiet the amygdala's alarm.

steps.label: 4 Touch · 3 Sound · 2 Smell
steps.cue: 4: Notice 4 physical sensations — the weight of your body, the texture of your clothing, the temperature of your hands, the floor under your feet. 3: Identify 3 distinct sounds in the environment. 2: Find 2 things you can smell, or recall 2 scents you know well.

steps.label: 1 Taste — Then Return
steps.cue: Notice 1 thing you can taste, or take a sip of water and pay full attention to the sensation. Then take 3 slow breaths. The full 5-4-3-2-1 protocol has been shown in clinical settings to reduce acute anxiety within 5 minutes by shifting attention from internal threat-monitoring to direct sensory experience.

sections.label: Somatic Grounding & Release
sections.purpose: Once the initial spike is addressed, these four techniques work deeper — using body awareness, gentle movement, and self-touch to consolidate the shift from fight-or-flight to safety. Each one communicates directly with the nervous system through proprioception, the breath, or social engagement cues.

exercises.name: Grounding Roots
exercises.start: Standing, feet hip-width, weight evenly distributed.
exercises.sub: Proprioceptive grounding through deliberate body awareness

steps.label: Feel the Ground
steps.cue: Stand barefoot if possible. Plant the feet firmly — feel the heel, the ball of the foot, and all five toes in contact with the floor. Press down slightly and feel the floor pressing back. This proprioceptive feedback tells the nervous system: you are standing on solid ground. You are not falling.

steps.label: Roots Down, Rise Up
steps.cue: Imagine roots growing downward from the soles of your feet — through the floor, into the earth. With each exhale, let the roots grow deeper. With each inhale, let the crown of the head rise slightly. The body lengthens in two directions. Anxiety contracts the body. This counters that.

steps.label: Breathe and Notice Weight
steps.cue: Feel the full weight of the body supported. The floor is holding you — you do not have to hold yourself up. Notice the hips, belly, chest — all simply resting on the skeletal frame, which is resting on the earth. Stay for the full duration. Anxiety requires you to be in your head; this practice puts you back in your body.

exercises.name: Forward Fold
exercises.start: Standing, feet hip-width, knees soft.
exercises.sub: Mild inversion activating baroreceptors, reducing sympathetic tone

steps.label: Hinge at the Hips
steps.cue: Stand with feet hip-width apart. Soften the knees generously. Hinge forward from the hips — not the waist — and let the upper body hang. Arms dangle. Head is heavy. If hamstrings pull, bend the knees more. The goal is full upper body release, not straight legs.

steps.label: Let the Head Hang Heavy
steps.cue: Let gravity take the weight of the skull. The mild inversion — head below heart — activates baroreceptors in the neck and chest. The brain interprets this as blood pressure rising and slows the heart to compensate. This is a direct cardiovascular pathway to calm. Breathe slowly.

steps.label: Sway and Release
steps.cue: Gently sway side to side — tiny movements, like seaweed in slow current. This bilateral movement, even small, helps discharge residual nervous energy. After 45–60 seconds, slowly roll up one vertebra at a time — head comes up last. Take 3 breaths at the top before continuing.

exercises.name: Hand on Heart
exercises.start: Seated or lying down, in any comfortable position.

steps.label: Place Both Hands
steps.cue: Place one or both hands flat on the center of the chest, over the heart. Close the eyes. Physically feel the warmth of your own hands through the fabric. This simple gesture activates the same neurological pathway as receiving care from another person — the body does not fully distinguish the source of the touch.

steps.label: Feel the Heartbeat
steps.cue: Press gently until you can feel your heartbeat — or simply feel the rise and fall of the chest with the breath. Acknowledge: this heart has been beating for your entire life without your asking it to. It does not need you to be anxious on its behalf. Let it do its work.

steps.label: Breathe Into the Hands
steps.cue: Breathe slowly, imagining the breath flowing to and from the hands. With each exhale, consciously soften the area beneath the palms. Dr. Kristin Neff's research on self-compassion shows this gesture measurably reduces cortisol and activates the caregiving system — the same system that calms an anxious child also calms an anxious adult.

exercises.name: Earlobe Circles
exercises.start: Seated, both hands raised to the ears.
exercises.sub: Auricular vagal branch stimulation through ear massage

steps.label: Find the Earlobes
steps.cue: Reach up and gently take both earlobes between the thumb and index finger. The ear — specifically the concha and tragus — contains a branch of the vagus nerve called the auricular branch. Gentle stimulation here has been shown in clinical studies to increase heart rate variability, a direct marker of parasympathetic tone.

steps.label: Slow Circles and Gentle Pulls
steps.cue: Make slow, gentle circles with the earlobes. Then gently pull the lobes downward — a slow, steady traction. Move to the outer ear rim (helix) and trace it slowly from bottom to top with the thumb. Take your time. This is not massage — it is gentle, deliberate stimulation.

steps.label: Continue and Breathe
steps.cue: Continue for the full minute, working both ears simultaneously. Breathe slowly — long exhales enhance the effect. You may notice a warmth in the ears, a softening of tension in the jaw, or a gentle heaviness behind the eyes. These are signs of vagal activation. This technique is used in integrative medicine for acute anxiety and insomnia.

stress.label: Tension Release
stress.subtitle: Stress, Relax, Unwind
stress.purpose: Complete the stress cycle the body started. Move the cortisol through. Then downshift.

sections.label: Physical Discharge
sections.purpose: Stress primes the body to fight or flee — cortisol and adrenaline flood the bloodstream, muscles tense, heart rate climbs. But modern stressors rarely let us move. These four exercises complete the stress cycle the body started: they give the nervous system the physical discharge it was primed for, which is the only pathway through — not around — the stress response.

exercises.name: Progressive Muscle Relaxation
exercises.start: Lying on your back on the floor or a firm surface, arms at sides.
exercises.sub: Systematic tension-release that discharges stored muscular stress

steps.label: Feet, Calves & Thighs
steps.cue: Starting at the feet: curl the toes and tense the feet hard for 5 seconds, then release completely. Next, contract the calves and thighs simultaneously — hold 5 seconds, then let go. The contrast between maximal tension and release is the mechanism. Stress stores muscular holding patterns below conscious awareness; PMR makes them visible and clears them.

steps.label: Core, Hands & Arms
steps.cue: Tense the glutes and core together — hold 5 seconds, release. Then make tight fists and tense the forearms, biceps, and shoulders all at once — hold 5 seconds, release fully. Let the arms go completely heavy. Notice the warmth in the released muscles — this is increased blood flow returning.

steps.label: Shoulders, Jaw & Face
steps.cue: Shrug the shoulders to the ears — hold 5 seconds, release. Clench the jaw and scrunch the face — hold 5 seconds, release. Finally, take a full breath, tense the entire body from feet to face simultaneously — hold 5 seconds, then release everything at once and exhale fully. One complete cycle is enough; repeat if time allows.

exercises.name: Rhythmic Walking
exercises.start: Standing, with enough space to walk or march in place.
exercises.sub: Bilateral gait cycle that metabolizes cortisol and adrenaline

steps.label: March in Place
steps.cue: Begin marching — knees lifting high, arms swinging in opposition (right arm forward as left knee rises). The gait cycle is the body's natural stress-metabolism mechanism. Research shows a brisk 10-minute walk reduces cortisol by 15–20%. Even 2 minutes of deliberate marching initiates this response.

steps.label: Cross-Body Pattern
steps.cue: Exaggerate the cross-body swing: right elbow drives back as the left knee lifts, and vice versa. This bilateral, alternating pattern activates both brain hemispheres simultaneously and has a measurable organizing effect on a dysregulated nervous system — the same mechanism that makes walking after an argument feel instinctively helpful.

steps.label: Audible Exhale with Each Step
steps.cue: Breathe out with each step — a short, deliberate exhale through the mouth, like punctuation. Make it audible. Lift the knees higher, let the arms swing wider. After 2 minutes you will notice a shift: the quality of mental activity changes. Stress rumination is harder to sustain when the body is engaged bilaterally.

exercises.name: Thoracic Extension
exercises.start: Seated in a firm chair, hands clasped behind the head.
exercises.sub: Opens the chest compressed by stress posture, restores full breathing

steps.label: Hands Behind Head
steps.cue: Sit at the edge of a firm chair. Interlace the fingers behind the head, elbows wide. Chronic stress collapses the chest inward and forward — shoulders round, the thoracic spine flexes, and the breath shortens. This position is the direct reversal of that pattern.

steps.label: Extend Over the Chair Back
steps.cue: Lean back gently over the top edge of the chair back — let it act as a fulcrum for the mid-thoracic spine. Allow the head to drop back, elbows flare open, chest lifts toward the ceiling. Hold 10–15 seconds. Breathe into the stretch. You may feel or hear gentle cracking — this is normal joint cavitation.

steps.label: Repeat at Different Levels
steps.cue: Come up slowly, shift position up slightly on the chair, and extend again at a different vertebral level. 3–4 extensions across the mid and upper thoracic. Each one opens the anterior chest, stretches the intercostals, and restores full lung volume — all of which are mechanically restricted during the stress posture.

exercises.name: The Shake-Out
exercises.start: Standing, feet hip-width, knees soft.
exercises.sub: TRE — Tension/Trauma Releasing Exercises via natural tremor

steps.label: Soft Knees, Begin Bouncing
steps.cue: Stand with feet hip-width, knees softly bent. Let the arms hang completely loose. Begin gentle heel-drops — heels barely lifting, dropping with gravity, letting the body bounce passively. Do not force anything. Animals in the wild tremble after a stressful encounter to discharge the activation; this is the same reflex.

steps.label: Let the Shake Travel Up
steps.cue: Let the tremoring move up from the feet — through the knees, hips, belly, chest, arms. If the body wants to shake more, let it. If natural tremor doesn't arrive, gentle deliberate bouncing is still effective. The goal is movement without muscular control — surrendering the body to its own discharge mechanism.

steps.label: Gradually Still, Then Rest
steps.cue: After 90 seconds, let the shaking slow naturally — do not stop it abruptly. Stand completely still for 20 seconds and notice the quality of the body: warmth, tingling, a sense of weight and softness. This is parasympathetic activation. Used in trauma-informed therapy worldwide (TRE, Somatic Experiencing).

sections.label: Release & Downshift
sections.purpose: After physical discharge, the nervous system is receptive to deep parasympathetic recovery. These four movements work through compression, posture, breath, and bilateral stimulation to consolidate the shift from reactive to resting — from stress to safety.

exercises.name: Child's Pose
exercises.start: Kneeling on the floor, knees wide, big toes together.
exercises.sub: Vagal toning through forward fold and abdominal compression

steps.label: Kneel and Fold Forward
steps.cue: Kneel with knees wide or together, big toes touching. Fold the torso forward over the thighs and rest the forehead on the floor, or on stacked hands or a folded blanket. Arms can extend forward or rest alongside the body. Let the entire torso go heavy.

steps.label: Abdomen Against Thighs
steps.cue: The mild compression of the abdomen against the thighs in this position applies gentle pressure on the gut wall — which in turn stimulates the vagus nerve via the enteric nervous system. This is part of why forward folds feel intrinsically calming. Allow each exhale to press the belly more firmly against the thighs.

steps.label: Breathe Into the Back Body
steps.cue: Direct the breath into the back: feel the ribs widen laterally on the inhale, then settle on the exhale. Stay for the full 90 seconds. If the mind returns to the stressor, acknowledge it and redirect to the sensation of the forehead on the ground. You do not have to solve anything right now.

exercises.name: Chin Tucks
exercises.start: Seated upright or standing against a wall.
exercises.sub: Cervical decompression that releases the suboccipital muscles

steps.label: Upright Posture
steps.cue: Sit or stand tall. During stress, the head juts forward as the body prepares for action — this compresses the upper cervical spine, restricts blood flow to the brain, and contributes to tension headaches. The chin tuck is a direct reset of this pattern. Think: 'tall spine, double chin.'

steps.label: Draw the Chin Straight Back
steps.cue: Without tilting the head, draw the chin straight back — creating a 'double chin.' The head should feel like it's gliding back on a level track, not tucking downward. Hold the retraction gently for 3–5 seconds. You should feel a mild stretch at the base of the skull — this is the suboccipital release.

steps.label: 10 Slow Reps
steps.cue: Release and repeat — 10 slow, deliberate reps. Each one creates traction in the upper cervical joints, decompresses the C1-C2 region, and releases the muscles that clamp tight under stress. Combined with slow nasal breathing throughout, this produces a rapid drop in perceived tension.

exercises.name: Wall Pushes
exercises.start: Standing an arm's length from a solid wall.
exercises.sub: Isometric exertion that completes the stress activation cycle

steps.label: Hands on Wall at Shoulder Height
steps.cue: Place both palms flat against the wall at shoulder height, arms nearly straight. Step the feet back slightly. The stress response contracts muscles and primes them for exertion — this exercise uses that primed energy deliberately rather than letting it sit unspent in the body.

steps.label: Push with Maximum Effort
steps.cue: Press into the wall as hard as you can — as if you intend to push it over. Engage the entire kinetic chain: push through the feet, brace the core, press with the chest, shoulders, and arms simultaneously. Hold 10 seconds of maximal effort. Breathe out slowly during the push.

steps.label: Release and Exhale Fully
steps.cue: Release suddenly and exhale completely. The abrupt shift from maximum tension to release signals the nervous system that the threat response is complete. Repeat 3–5 times. This is the physical completion of the fight-or-flight cycle — the body gets the exertion it was primed to perform, and can now down-regulate.

exercises.name: Butterfly Hug
exercises.start: Seated or standing, arms crossed over the chest.
exercises.sub: Bilateral stimulation (EMDR technique) that integrates the stress response

steps.label: Cross Arms Over Chest
steps.cue: Cross both arms over the chest — right hand to the left shoulder area, left hand to the right — like hugging yourself. Fingertips rest near the collarbones or upper arms. Close the eyes. This position was developed by Lucina Artigas for trauma survivors and is a core technique in EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing) therapy.

steps.label: Alternating Taps
steps.cue: Begin alternating gentle taps — right hand taps, then left hand taps — at a slow, rhythmic pace, like a slow heartbeat. The alternating bilateral stimulation mimics REM sleep processing and activates both brain hemispheres simultaneously. This helps the nervous system integrate and file the stress experience rather than holding it.

steps.label: Continue and Breathe
steps.cue: Continue for the full 90 seconds. Breathe slowly and naturally. You may notice images, sensations, or memories arising — let them pass without engagement. The bilateral rhythm is doing the work. Many people find this position produces an immediate sense of comfort and safety — the same circuits activated by human contact are recruited through self-administered bilateral touch.

feet.label: Feet First
feet.subtitle: Fascia, Load, Release
feet.purpose: PT-backed protocol for plantar fasciitis and foot pain. Release the tissue, wake the muscles, load the chain.

sections.label: Release
sections.purpose: Start here, especially on pain days. The plantar fascia and calf complex are often tight and compressed before you've taken your first step. These movements soften the tissue and restore blood flow before asking anything of it.

exercises.name: Plantar Fascia Stretch
exercises.start: Seated in a chair, one foot crossed over the opposite knee.

steps.label: Seated Setup
steps.cue: Sit in a chair. Cross one foot over the opposite knee so the sole faces you. Hold the base of the toes with one hand. This is the windlass mechanism — the same tissue that loads with every walking step.

steps.label: Extend the Toes
steps.cue: Gently pull all toes back toward the shin until you feel a firm stretch along the arch. You should feel the plantar fascia tighten into a cord-like band. Hold this position — do not bounce. 30 seconds minimum. Research shows this specific stretch reduces morning pain more effectively than calf stretching alone.

steps.label: Massage the Arch
steps.cue: While holding the toe extension with one hand, use the thumb of the other hand to apply firm, slow strokes along the arch from heel to ball of foot. 5–6 strokes per set. Switch feet.

exercises.name: Seated Foot Roll
exercises.start: Seated in a chair, a ball or bottle under one foot.

steps.label: Find Your Tool
steps.cue: A frozen water bottle is ideal — the cold provides additional anti-inflammatory benefit. A tennis ball or smooth water bottle at room temperature also works. Place it on the floor in front of your chair.

steps.label: Roll the Arch
steps.cue: Place the arch of one foot on the bottle. Apply moderate downward pressure — enough to feel it, not enough to cause sharp pain. Slowly roll from the heel to the ball of the foot and back. 30 seconds per foot. Pause on any tender spots for 5–10 seconds rather than rolling through them.

steps.label: Target the Heel
steps.cue: Shift pressure toward the heel — specifically the medial tubercle of the calcaneus, where the fascia attaches. This is typically the most sensitive spot. Use lighter pressure here. Slow, sustained pressure is more effective than rolling speed.

exercises.name: Gastroc Stretch
exercises.start: Standing, hands on a wall, one foot stepped back.

steps.label: Wall Setup
steps.cue: Stand facing a wall, hands flat at shoulder height for support. Step one foot back about 18–24 inches, keeping both feet pointing straight forward. The back knee stays completely straight throughout — this targets the gastrocnemius, the larger calf muscle that crosses the knee.

steps.label: Lunge Forward
steps.cue: Bend the front knee and shift your weight forward until you feel a deep stretch in the calf of the back leg. Keep the back heel firmly on the floor — if it lifts, step the foot closer. Hold for 30–45 seconds. The gastroc is directly connected to the Achilles tendon, which loads the plantar fascia.

steps.label: Switch & Repeat
steps.cue: Switch sides. If one side is significantly tighter, spend an extra round there. Tight calves are among the most common contributing factors to plantar fasciitis — they increase load on the fascia with every step.

exercises.name: Soleus Stretch
exercises.start: Standing at a wall, back knee bent, rear heel down.

steps.label: Wall Setup
steps.cue: Same starting position as the Gastroc Stretch — one foot stepped back, hands on wall. But this time, bend the back knee slightly. This small change completely shifts the stretch from the gastrocnemius to the soleus — the deeper, shorter calf muscle that attaches below the knee.

steps.label: Sink Into It
steps.cue: With the back knee bent, shift weight gently forward. The stretch will feel lower and deeper than the gastroc stretch — closer to the Achilles and heel. Keep the back heel on the floor. Hold 30–45 seconds. The soleus is often the tighter of the two calf muscles and the more overlooked.

steps.label: Switch Sides
steps.cue: Repeat on the other side. If you have one-sided plantar fasciitis, pay particular attention to the soleus on that side. This stretch is often prescribed alone for cases where standard calf stretching hasn't helped.

sections.label: Activation
sections.purpose: Years of supportive footwear have switched off the intrinsic muscles of the foot — the small muscles that create and maintain the arch. These exercises wake them back up. This is not quick or dramatic work. It is foundational.

exercises.name: Toe Splay
exercises.start: Seated or standing barefoot on a firm surface.

steps.label: Seated or Standing
steps.cue: Sit or stand barefoot on a firm surface. Place both feet flat on the floor, parallel, hip-width apart. Relax everything from the ankle down — no gripping or curling of toes. Feel the full footprint on the floor.

steps.label: Spread All Toes
steps.cue: Actively spread all five toes as wide apart as possible without lifting the foot. Hold for 2 seconds. Most people discover they cannot independently move their toes much at all — this is normal and is exactly why this exercise exists. The abductor hallucis and the interossei are the target muscles.

steps.label: Release & Repeat
steps.cue: Slowly release the spread. Pause 1 second. Repeat. If only some toes move, that is useful information — work with what you have. Over days and weeks, the range and independence improves noticeably.

exercises.name: Arch Doming
exercises.start: Seated, one foot flat on the floor.
exercises.sub: Short foot exercise — intrinsic arch activation

steps.label: Setup
steps.cue: Sit with one foot flat on the floor. The goal is to shorten the foot from heel to ball — creating a dome shape in the arch — without curling the toes. This is called the short foot exercise. It directly activates the flexor digitorum brevis and abductor hallucis, the primary arch-supporting muscles.

steps.label: Draw Heel to Ball
steps.cue: Without lifting the heel or curling the toes, try to drag the ball of the foot toward the heel. The arch should visibly rise. Hold for 3 seconds. This movement is subtle and mentally demanding. If the toes curl, relax them — the arch lift must come from the intrinsic muscles, not the long toe flexors.

steps.label: Release & Build
steps.cue: Slowly release the arch. 10 reps per foot. As this becomes easier, progress to performing it standing, then standing on one leg. The short foot exercise is the single most prescribed intrinsic foot strengthening movement in sports PT and orthopedic rehabilitation.

exercises.name: Big Toe Extension
exercises.start: Seated, foot flat on the floor.

steps.label: Seated Setup
steps.cue: Sit with foot flat on the floor. Place two fingers lightly under the big toe. The big toe is the most mechanically important digit in walking — its extension during push-off is what tightens the plantar fascia like a winch (the windlass mechanism). Restricted big toe extension is a direct cause of arch strain.

steps.label: Lift Only the Big Toe
steps.cue: Lift only the big toe as high as possible, keeping the other four toes on the floor. Hold for 2 seconds. Then reverse — press the big toe down and lift the other four. This isolation trains the extensor hallucis and establishes independent toe control.

steps.label: Assisted Extension
steps.cue: Using your fingers, gently assist the big toe into full extension — past where it goes on its own. Hold for 5 seconds. This passively mobilizes the first metatarsophalangeal joint and stretches the intrinsic flexors. Restricted range here correlates directly with plantar fasciitis severity.

sections.label: Strengthening
sections.purpose: Eccentric loading — slow, controlled lengthening under tension — has the strongest evidence base of any intervention for plantar fasciitis and Achilles tendinopathy. These are not stretches. The calf-heel complex must work under load to remodel the tissue.

exercises.name: Eccentric Heel Drops
exercises.start: Standing on the edge of a step, heels hanging off the back.

steps.label: Step Edge Setup
steps.cue: Stand on the edge of a step or curb with both feet, heels hanging off the back. Rise up onto both forefeet to the top position. This is the Alfredson protocol — the most evidence-backed exercise for plantar fasciitis and Achilles tendinopathy. Keep the working knee straight throughout.

steps.label: Single-Leg Lower
steps.cue: Shift weight to one foot. Slowly lower the heel below the step level — taking a full 3 seconds to descend. You are loading the calf eccentrically: it is contracting while lengthening. This is the specific stimulus that promotes tendon and fascial remodeling. Do not rush.

steps.label: Two-Leg Return
steps.cue: Use both feet to rise back to the top position. Switch the working leg. 15 reps each side. If this is painless, add load over time — a backpack with a book is sufficient. Mild discomfort during the movement is acceptable; sharp pain is not.

exercises.name: Eccentric Soleus Drops
exercises.start: Standing on the edge of a step, heels hanging off the back.
exercises.sub: Bent-knee heel drops — deeper calf loading

steps.label: Step Edge, Knee Bent
steps.cue: Same starting position as Eccentric Heel Drops — on a step edge, heels hanging. But this time, bend the working knee to approximately 30–40 degrees. This simple change shifts the load from the gastrocnemius to the soleus. The soleus attaches below the knee and is the primary load-bearing muscle of the calf during walking.

steps.label: Slow Descent
steps.cue: Lower the heel slowly on the bent-knee leg — 3 full seconds down. Maintain the same knee angle throughout the descent. Do not let the knee straighten during the lowering phase or you shift back to the gastrocnemius. Feel the load deep in the lower calf, just above the heel.

steps.label: Two-Leg Return
steps.cue: Use both feet to return to the top. Switch sides. 15 reps each side. These two eccentric exercises — straight knee and bent knee — together cover the full calf-Achilles-fascia chain. Done consistently 3 days a week, clinical improvement is typically measurable within 6–8 weeks.

exercises.name: Towel Scrunches
exercises.start: Seated barefoot, a small towel flat under one foot.

steps.label: Towel Setup
steps.cue: Sit barefoot with a small towel flat on the floor under one foot. The towel should be thin enough that you can feel the floor texture through it. No towel? Use a sock or work on a textured surface — the goal is toe flexion against mild resistance.

steps.label: Scrunch & Hold
steps.cue: Curl all five toes to scrunch the towel toward you. Use only the toes — do not slide the whole foot. Hold the scrunch for 2 seconds, then release fully. This targets the flexor digitorum brevis and the lumbricals — the small muscles that support the metatarsal arch, the often-overlooked transverse arch of the foot.

steps.label: Repeat & Switch
steps.cue: Continue for 20 seconds, then switch feet. 3 sets per side. As strength builds, progress to standing on one leg while performing the scrunch — this adds the balance challenge and integrates the intrinsic work into functional loading.

knee.label: Knee Resilience
knee.subtitle: Knee, Mobility, Strength
knee.purpose: Bulletproof the joint: strengthen the muscles that support it and use low-load movements to lubricate and offload pressure.

sections.label: Knee Resilience
sections.purpose: Bulletproof the joint by strengthening the muscles that support it while using low-load, high-precision movements to lubricate the joint and offload pressure. Motion is lotion — it's normal for arthritic or cranky knees to feel stiff for the first few repetitions; as the joint warms up and synovial fluid flows, discomfort usually gives way to ease.

exercises.name: Wall-Press TKE (Terminal Knee Extension)
exercises.start: Standing, facing a wall with one foot slightly behind the other. The front knee is slightly bent.

steps.label: Wall Setup
steps.cue: Stand facing a wall. Place one foot slightly behind the other. The front knee is slightly bent. Keep the front heel grounded.

steps.label: Press & Lock Out
steps.cue: Press the front heel into the floor and squeeze the quadriceps hard to 'lock out' the knee. Hold the squeeze for 2 seconds.

steps.label: Release & Repeat
steps.cue: Release and repeat 10–12 times per leg. Switch sides.

exercises.name: Straight Leg Raises
exercises.start: Lying on your back: one leg bent with foot flat, the other leg straight out.

steps.label: Supine Setup
steps.cue: Lie on your back. One leg bent, foot flat; the other leg extended straight.

steps.label: Lift to Opposite Knee Height
steps.cue: Lift the straight leg to the height of the opposite bent knee. Squeeze the quad at the top. Lower slowly.

steps.label: Controlled Lower
steps.cue: Lower in 3 seconds. 10–12 reps per leg.

exercises.name: Isometric Wall Sits
exercises.start: Standing: back flat against a wall, feet shoulder-width apart and 12 inches forward.

steps.label: Wall Contact
steps.cue: Stand with back flat against a wall. Feet shoulder-width, about 12 inches in front.

steps.label: Slide Down
steps.cue: Slide down into a partial squat. Hold. Isometric holds have a pain-numbing effect on joints.

steps.label: Breathe
steps.cue: Hold 45 seconds. Breathe normally.

exercises.name: Towel Heel Slides
exercises.start: Lying on your back: one heel resting on a towel or smooth surface.

steps.label: Supine, Heel on Towel
steps.cue: Lie on your back. One heel on a towel or smooth floor.

steps.label: Slide Heel Toward Glutes
steps.cue: Slowly slide the heel toward your glutes, then back out. Joint oiling for stiffness.

steps.label: Smooth & Slow
steps.cue: Continue 45 seconds. Repeat other side if time allows.

exercises.name: Self-Resisted Clamshells
exercises.start: Lying on your side: knees bent at 90 degrees and stacked. Top hand on outside of top knee.
exercises.sub: Hip strength to prevent knee cave

steps.label: Side-Lying Setup
steps.cue: Lie on your side. Knees bent 90°, stacked. Top hand on outside of top knee.

steps.label: Lift Against Resistance
steps.cue: Lift the top knee while pressing down with your hand. Stronger hips prevent the knee from caving inward.

steps.label: Lower & Repeat
steps.cue: Lower slowly. 10–12 reps per side.

exercises.name: Seated Knee Extensions
exercises.start: Seated: sitting tall in a chair with feet dangling.

steps.label: Chair Setup
steps.cue: Sit tall in a chair, feet dangling. Hands on chair or thighs.

steps.label: Straighten Leg
steps.cue: Slowly straighten one leg until horizontal. Squeeze the quad at the top. Maintains thigh muscle mass when weight-bearing is painful.

steps.label: Lower Slowly
steps.cue: Lower in 3 seconds. 10–12 reps per leg.

exercises.name: Low Box Step-Ups
exercises.start: Standing: facing a low step or the bottom stair.

steps.label: Step Setup
steps.cue: Stand facing a low step or bottom stair. 4–6 inches is enough to start.

steps.label: Step Up
steps.cue: Step up slowly with one foot. Bring the other foot to meet it.

steps.label: Slow Descent
steps.cue: Step down slowly — 3–4 seconds to lower. Builds eccentric strength that protects the knee. 8–10 reps each leg.
